Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% For each masseuse M, they schedule exactly one client C and one price P.
{schedule(M, C, P): clients(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
diplomats("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% For each diplomat D, assign exactly 1 month M and 1 duration T.
{assign(D, M, T): months(M), duration(T)}=1 :- diplomats(D).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").

% For each name R, it launches at exactly 1 month M and with exactly 1 company C.
{launch(R, C, M): months(M), companies(C)}=1 :- names(R).
```

% For each witness W, it is associated with exactly one date D and one town T.

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
packsize(25; 30; 35; 40).
brands("Adironda"; "Grennel"; "Naturba"; "Travelore").
names("Arthur"; "Eugene"; "Natasha"; "Olga").

% Each camper (name) is assigned exactly one packsize and one brand.
{assign(N, S, B): packsize(S), brands(B)}=1 :- names(N).

% Clue 1: Arthur's pack is either 25L or Adironda.
:- assign("Arthur", S, B), S != 25, B != "Adironda".

% Clue 2: Eugene's pack is made by Adironda.
:- assign("Eugene", S, B), B != "Adironda".

% Clue 3: Natasha's pack is either 40L or Travelore.
:- assign("Natasha", S, B), S != 40, B != "Travelore".

% Clue 4: Natasha's pack is 10L larger than the Grennel pack.
% Find the size of Natasha's pack and the size of the Grennel pack.
% They must differ by 10.
:- assign("Natasha", S1, _), assign(_, S2, "Grennel"), S1 != S2 + 10.

% Clue 5: The 30L pack is made by Grennel.
:- assign(_, 30, B), B != "Grennel".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employees(1; 2; 3; 4).
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("green"; "pink"; "purple"; "red").

% Define the assignment predicate: each employee is assigned one rider count and one section.
assign(E, R, S) :- employees(E), riders(R), section(S).

% Each employee is assigned exactly one rider and one section.
{assign(E, R, S) : riders(R), section(S)} = 1 :- employees(E).

% Each rider count is assigned to exactly one employee.
{assign(E, R, S) : employees(E), section(S)} = 1 :- riders(R).

% Each section is assigned to exactly one employee.
{assign(E, R, S) : employees(E), riders(R)} = 1 :- section(S).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
runners("Franklin"; "Salvador"; "Ted"; "Zachary").
time(21; 22; 23; 24).
colors("aquamarine"; "black"; "cyan"; "maroon").

% Each runner is assigned exactly one time and one color.
{run(R, T, C): time(T), colors(C)}=1 :- runners(R).

% Clue 1: Zachary wore the aquamarine shirt.
run(Zachary, T, "aquamarine") :- time(T).

% Clue 2: The contestant in the cyan shirt finished 1 minute after Franklin.
run(Franklin, T1, _) :- time(T1).
run(R, T2, "cyan") :- runners(R), time(T2), T2 = T1 + 1.

% Clue 3: Zachary finished 2 minutes after Salvador.
run(Salvador, T, _) :- time(T).
run(Zachary, T2, _) :- time(T2), T2 = T + 2.

% Clue 4: The contestant in the maroon shirt finished 1 minute before Salvador.
run(Salvador, T, _) :- time(T).
run(R, T2, "maroon") :- runners(R), time(T2), T2 = T - 1.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
fliers("Katie"; "Neal"; "Troy"; "Yolanda").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
lucky_charms("coin"; "rabbit's foot"; "talisman"; "wishbone").

% Define the ternary predicate to represent the assignment: flier F leaves in month M with lucky charm C.
flight(F, M, C) :- fliers(F), months(M), lucky_charms(C).

% Each flier is assigned exactly one month and one lucky charm.
{flight(F, M, C) : months(M), lucky_charms(C)} = 1 :- fliers(F).

% Each month is used by exactly one flier.
{flight(F, M, C) : fliers(F), lucky_charms(C)} = 1 :- months(M).

% Each lucky charm is used by exactly one flier.
{flight(F, M, C) : fliers(F), months(M)} = 1 :- lucky_charms(C).

% Clue 1: The passenger with the wishbone is either Troy or Neal.
:- not (flight(Troy, _, "wishbone") ; flight(Neal, _, "wishbone")).

% Clue 2: The four fliers are the passenger with the rabbit's foot, the passenger leaving in April, Yolanda, and the passenger leaving in February.
% This implies that the set of fliers is exactly the union of these four roles.
% So: the rabbit's foot carrier is one of the fliers (already true), etc.
% But more importantly, it means that every flier is described by one of these attributes.
% We encode this by ensuring that:
% - The flier with rabbit's foot is one of the four.
% - The April flier is one of the four.
% - Yolanda is one of the four.
% - The February flier is one of the four.
% These are all inherently satisfied by the domain, so we only need to ensure that:
% The rabbit's foot carrier is among the fliers (true), etc.
% But to enforce that these four roles are distinct and cover all fliers, we can use:
% The person with rabbit's foot must be one of the fliers → always true.
% Instead, the clue implies that the four roles are the only fliers, so we must ensure:
% - The person with rabbit's foot is one of the fliers → true.
% - The April flier is one of the fliers → true.
% - Yolanda is one of the fliers → true.
% - The February flier is one of the fliers → true.
% But more importantly, it implies that no flier is outside these roles.
% So: every flier must be either:
%   - the rabbit's foot carrier, or
%   - the April flier, or
%   - Yolanda, or
%   - the February flier.
% This is encoded as:
% For each flier F, F must be the rabbit's foot carrier OR the April flier OR Yolanda OR the February flier.
:- fliers(F), not (flight(F, _, "rabbit's foot")), not (flight(F, 4, _)), not (F = "Yolanda"), not (flight(F, 2, _)).
```
